taskVersion.name: epu_q2_long
taskVersion.definition:
@kbench.task(name="epu_q2_long")
def epu_q2_long(
    llm,
    article_key: str,
    content_for_eval: str,
    gold_epu: int,
    short_pre: str = "",
    short_post: str = "",
    medium_pre: str = "",
    medium_post: str = "",
    long_pre: str = "",
    long_post: str = "",
    disagreement_flag: int = 0,
) -> float:
    out = model_json_call(llm, build_q2_prompt(content_for_eval, long_pre, long_post))
    m = binary_match(out["epu"], gold_epu)
    return 0.0 if m is None else float(m)

parts.text:
You are evaluating only the text between TARGET ARTICLE START and TARGET ARTICLE END.

Task:
Decide whether the TARGET ARTICLE expresses ECONOMIC POLICY UNCERTAINTY (EPU).

Labeling rule:
- EPU = 1 only if the TARGET ARTICLE discusses uncertainty about government policy,
  policy decisions, possible policy actions, political outcomes affecting policy,
  or the economic effects of policy.
- EPU = 0 for general economics, elections, markets, war, uncertainty, or foreign events
  unless the uncertainty is specifically tied to economic policy.

IMPORTANT:
- Use only the text inside TARGET ARTICLE START and TARGET ARTICLE END for the decision.
- Return ONLY valid JSON.
- No markdown. No explanation. No code fence.

Return exactly these keys:
{
  "epu": 0 or 1,
  "confidence": number between 0 and 1,
  "focus_excerpt": "short quote from target article only"
}

TARGET ARTICLE START
The party of President Slobodan Milosevic claimed Sunday he was on his way to a decisive victory in elections marred by allegations of widespread fraud. An opposition leader said the Milosevic claim was ``upside down'' and that the Serbian strongman would be ousted by his leading opponent. Official results were not announced as thousands of opposition supporters celebrated in a downtown Belgrade square where riot police were withdrawn after an early show of force. Going into Sunday's election, democratic challenger Vojislav Kostunica had a lead in opinion polls and, absent fraud, appeared poised to do what three military defeats in 10 years could not: remove Milosevic from office. Milosevic's army chief of staff pledged to snuff out anyone in the democratic opposition who tried to take power by force. To avoid an Oct. 8 runoff, one of the two major presidential candidates needed to win more than 50 percent of the vote, the precise margin Milosevic's party said he had achieved. With foreign monitors and reporters largely absent from Serbia, Western observers said the election was Milosevic's to steal. ``We had hundreds and hundreds of irregularities reported in hundreds and hundreds of polling sites'' across Serbia, said Marko Blagojevic of the Center for Free Elections and Democracy, a 3-year-old independent Belgrade group. ``These elections were not organized in a fair fashion.'' Election stakes were high for the isnited States and Western Europe, which have imposed economic

[...TRUNCATED...]

n out of voting establishments across the country during the day. Some were let in only after the voting was completed and ballot boxes were sealed. ``In other places there was not private voting but public voting - they had to vote in the open, in front of the election board,'' he said. With Montenegro officially boycotting the federal elections, and the province of Kosovo readying for its own races, Milosevic, 59, ltoked this summer to have an easy battle. But Kostunica, a 56-year-old Belgrade law professor, began climbing in independent polls over the last two months, and the bickering opposition closed ranks, mounting the most formidable challenge yet to Milosevic. With the balloting concluded but the outcome uncertain, Yugoslavs wondered what would happen next. ``I don't think anyone knows this answer,'' says Milka Tadic, editor of The Monitor, a decade-old independent weekly published in Podgorica. ``If [Milosevic] wins the election, and I think he will - he will steal it - he has to keep his public busy with something. ``It might be a crisis in Montenegro. If he steals the election, I'm sure there will be some protest in Serbia. Whenever he had troubles before, he would cause a crisis somewhere else, like Bosnia or Croatia.'' Caption: photo: Yugoslav President Slobodan Milosevic and wife Mirjana Markovic vote in Belgrade (a) ALEKSANDAR ANDJIC/AP PARTNERS: Yugoslav President Slobodan Milosevic and wife, Mirjana Markovic, vote in Belgrade.
TARGET ARTICLE END
